text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.

text: Welcome to the show.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Hey everybody, today we're going to talk about why I feel everybody should learn some type of technology that is really heavily rooted in the unidirectional data flow pattern.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm going to recommend that you learn something like React.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if you still can't stomach the thought of learning something in React, maybe you're not a web developer, you don't have that experience.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Then I recommend that you learn something like Flutter, which has a very similar concept to React.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: One of the things you'll notice when you're working with Flutter, if you go down that route, and if you do have experience with React, is it feels very much like React.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and what the thing that you're feeling that is similar from flutter to react and so forth is the unit directional data flow pattern and so today's short episode is going to talk a little bit about that which is the unit directional data flow pattern and why you should be implementing that inside of perhaps some of your apps or just experience it with something like react or flutter
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's just stick with the React concept because that was my first real...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've worked with unidirectional data flow before, but when I finally got to develop a React app, probably about three years ago, it's where everything really clicked for me.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I thought, oh, this is how it should be done.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Everything else that I'd worked with inside of Android was kind of this hodgepodge.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Everything's kind of bolted together.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There wasn't any really cohesive way of implementing it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: though you can do it, it would never felt like it was supposed to be that way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I always felt like things were just kind of patched together and it was very easy to miss a step or just fall back into old bad patterns or habits.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When you work with React, React works a very certain way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And essentially what happens is, let's just take it from, again, if you're not familiar with React, let's cover that for a second.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: React is a web UI framework.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's for creating basically single page applications.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and you have state, you have views, and you have actions that you can perform and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the general synopsis is that state is passed into a view and into child components.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Inside of React, you have components.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These are basically things that are shown on the screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It could be a list item.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It could be part of a screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It could be the whole screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're all just components.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In Android, we'd call those views.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so state is passed to each of these views.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then state from there can also be passed to child components.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So components can have children components.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then certain actions are triggered by the view.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This could be typing in some text that could be clicking a button, anything like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then these actions can then update that state.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, what happens is behind the scenes, React is watching for any of these state changes and will re-render the page
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and redraw everything out in real time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So there's kind of like this reactive flow to it, which is why it's called React.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The state then when it's changed is passed to the view then into its child component.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if we have perhaps some state that's showing a name and it is displaying in a couple of children components and the parent component has a input box with a button and you type into this input box the name or you change a name,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: those change events inside of that input box could then update the state and which then at that time react could then update all the child components as well because it's watching in real time and you really don't have to do anything you don't have to tell react to do this it just does it
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: As long as you're passing the state down and using the props and everything as React has been designed and you're not fighting the system, all of this will just happen automatically.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You don't have to wire anything up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You don't have to subscribe.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You don't have to do anything of that nature whatsoever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you click a button, you can then, maybe if you decide to click a button, it turns the text to uppercase.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You could do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, what's happening here is the state is passed to the view, which if you have child components, it's just showing the first name.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're then changing the text, that's an action that's being triggered by the view.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When that action occurs, which is a change event, it's updating the state, you're updating the name variable or whatever, the name state variable.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And at that point, React sees that the state has been changed and it's gonna update the view and all of its child components that have access or are using
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: that data.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so again, we're going from, we have the state, which is shown in the view and the view has actions and the actions can update the state, which then in turn updates the view.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's kind of a nice circular process here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's unidirectional, meaning it's one way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We're always going to go one way.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if you think about it, the view is really the result of the application state and state can only change when actions happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then when actions happen, the state is updated.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now there's some benefits with this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is known as a one-way binding.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's not a two-way binding.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this has some really high end, well not high end, just some really key advantages that you can lean on here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And one of them is gonna be that it's less error prone.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have basically more control over your data because it's easier to debug.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You really know where your data is coming from and where.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When something's being updated, it's being passed down.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Inside of a React component,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: very often you'll have some props and these are basically values that are passed into your component.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let's say you receive a name variable in through the constructor props, which is basically the constructor of that component.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If that changes, React will know that that changes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Perhaps it was changed from the parent.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: React will kind of walk down the entire object graph and know what state has been changed and where it's come from.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you're actually working inside of a component and you're wondering, why is this thing updating or not updating?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can then go find it inside of your
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: component.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Is it coming from your props or is it not coming from your props?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: As long as you understand that data is always flowing down from the top, it's much easier to understand.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now it's also much more efficient because the react library knows the boundaries of each part of the system.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It knows that it's always pushing that data down.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So overall, you know that where the data is coming from and it's some type of state change is happening.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's updating the state, which then is just flowing through the system.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And one thing to note here too is when you have a unidirectional data flow pattern, it's important to note that changing a state on like a React component will never really affect its parent, unless you have some type of callback mechanism that's kind of built in through an action.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But if we just leave that out of it, anytime state changes in a component, it won't affect its parent or its direct siblings inside of the hierarchy of the DOM.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: or any other component of the application.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It'll just affect its children.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if you do want it to affect some type of higher level, then you need some type of state management technique that could be something like Redux.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It could be something like a reducer somewhere, or you could have some type of callbacks.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a bunch of different options.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, what you'll see is that there's different ways to accomplish this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, you do have to be careful with that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There have been multiple...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You see multiple blog posts about humongous Redux applications where it's just a complete mess to manage and basically it's a huge state machine.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you have to be really careful of what state is going where.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've built applications where I don't have any state that's managed by the application at the higher level and components are just rendering data that they need inside of their own components.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, one of the things you might be thinking is, well, what if I need to get data from an API?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, that's fine.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: A component can make HTTP calls and it goes out and gets data and then it renders the data on the screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that component may have child components and it pushes its data down into those child components and it updates.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The same thing could also happen when an action occurs, maybe in a child component.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You say, hey, this button was clicked and you just provide a callback.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then the parent can say, hey, when this child's button is clicked, call this parent action.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you basically pass this in through a, pass the function in through the constructor.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So passing in a function that gets re-invoked and maybe that function just calls back out to the database to, or to the API to refresh the data.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, the data is refreshed in the parent.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and shove back down to the children.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So overall,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The real key thing here that you're gonna learn when you're learning React is how the unidirectional data flow pattern works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And because React is built a certain way, it only works that way really.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you try to fight against it or you wanna try to inject different types of things, you're gonna get some real messy spaghetti code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But if you kind of follow the patterns and the configuration of how React is set up, it's actually very elegant and you will learn a lot about how unidirectional data flow works
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and actually about how powerful React is.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, I did mention earlier on that Flutter is a valuable tool to also learn this, and I still believe that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In my opinion, and I haven't interviewed anyone over on the Flutter team, but it is quite obvious that Flutter is highly, highly influenced by React.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, the same thing can also be said of something like Jetpack Compose or SwiftUI.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Both have this style of unidirectional data flow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the reason why is just because it works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's actually kind of a joy to work with, and you're not having spaghetti code all over the place and event buses and all kinds of stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, again, that doesn't mean that it's protecting you from writing bad code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can always do that, but it does show you the, gives you a nice system.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's just built in to the platform in which you can update state to update the view and then perform actions to update that state.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's just kind of goes in the circular pattern.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what I'm going to do inside of the show notes is I'm going to include an image in the show notes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So be sure to check that out and you'll see a nice little
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: kind of a circular diagram that shows the view, the actions, the state, and I'll put some notes inside of the show notes so you can view it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'll also provide a couple of links for you if you're interested in learning React or how you can get started.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a couple of places out there that you can learn React pretty easily, and the documentation's pretty good.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the same thing for Flutter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: you're gonna learn on both ends what and how unidirectional dataflow works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if you do go with React, there's a couple of different ways to do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're gonna have hooks, you're gonna have, you know, function components, stuff like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can use whichever one really makes sense to you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I choose to use hooks just because it makes sense and it's a lot less code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you're using Flutter, you'll use basically the set state all over the place.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And now again, once you kind of get into each one of these platforms, you're going to see patterns inside of there that you haven't seen inside of other technologies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It doesn't mean you have to go into it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It doesn't mean you have to become an expert.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All you're really doing here is learning about how the unit directional data flow pattern works simply because it helps you be less error prone and have more control over your data.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's easier to debug because you know exactly where that data is coming from.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And overall, it just makes you much more efficient, in my opinion, as a developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to see this pattern and it just makes sense and wonder like, wow, how did I live without this before?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Which, again, is one of my opinions of why things like Jetpack Compose, SwiftUI are going to be very popular once they get to a stable state.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: These things have massive, massive opportunities to make the developer experience for both platforms a lot better.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if you do decide to go to web, you can just kind of hop over to something like React or one of the other libraries that are similar to it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I hope that helps.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, check the show notes for the diagram and the links so you can understand how and what you need to do to experiment with one of these things.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I know it sounds simple, but when you have to-dos in there, there's a bunch of things you have to deal with.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: How are you gonna update state?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: How are you gonna store that state?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Are you gonna communicate with an API?

text: Are you gonna use something custom like your own database?

text: Are you gonna use Firebase?

speaker: SPEAKER_00
